seoTitle: How to Change DNS Records in Cloudflare (2026)
metaDescription: Edit or add DNS records in Cloudflare, from updating existing A and CNAME entries to setting proxy status and TTL correctly.
intro: Whether you're correcting a mistake or updating routing for a new service, changing DNS records in Cloudflare follows a consistent pattern across record types. This guide covers editing existing entries and adding new ones through the DNS Records panel.

steps.heading: Open the DNS Records page
steps.body: Select your domain in Cloudflare and open the refreshed DNS Records management grid.

steps.heading: Edit an existing record
steps.body: Click Edit next to any row to instantly modify its IP address or destination string.

steps.heading: Or add a new record
steps.body: Click '+ Add Record' to open a fresh configuration line for A, CNAME, or MX entries.

steps.heading: Set the record type and values
steps.body: Select the correct type and enter the appropriate hostname, subdomain, or target value.

steps.heading: Adjust proxy status, TTL, and save
steps.body: Toggle proxy status between orange cloud and gray cloud depending on whether you want Cloudflare's protection active, set your TTL, and save to deploy the change.

faq.question: How quickly do DNS changes take effect in Cloudflare?
faq.answer: Changes within Cloudflare's own network typically apply almost immediately, though full global propagation to all resolvers can take longer.

faq.question: What's the risk of editing an existing record instead of adding a new one?
faq.answer: Editing overwrites the existing entry, so double-check the current value before saving if you might need to reference it later.

faq.question: When should a record be proxied versus DNS Only?
faq.answer: Proxying enables Cloudflare's caching and security for web traffic, while DNS Only routes traffic directly, which is required for things like mail records.

faq.question: Can I undo a DNS change after saving it?
faq.answer: You can manually re-enter the previous value to revert a change, so it's worth noting the original settings before editing.
